prevalence.value: 10-20% (adults; higher in older adults and women)
prevalence.trend: increasing with screen time, aging, and environmental factors

riskFactors: screen time and low blink rate
vulnerablePopulations: office workers with high screen time
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Symptoms plus objective signs (TBUT, staining, Schirmer) where available; rule out infection and blepharitis contributors

publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate; improves with lubricants and addressing blepharitis/meibomian dysfunction

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Often diagnosed clinically; objective testing more available in ophthalmology services
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Artificial tears, environmental modifications, treat blepharitis, topical anti-inflammatories in selected cases
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Cost/access to lubricants and specialist care in some regions
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Most management is low-risk and can be initiated in primary care with referral as needed
